children.name: Varanus
children.description: Large to enormous predatory lizards with deeply forked tongues, powerful claws, and muscular bodies. Monitors actively hunt prey rather than ambushing it, using their long forked tongue to track scent trails. The Komodo Dragon is the largest lizard on Earth, capable of killing water buffalo.

children.name: Varanus komodoensis
children.description: World's largest living lizard at up to 3 m and 70 kg; venomous — venom glands disrupt blood clotting; capable of parthenogenesis; restricted to a handful of Indonesian islands; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Varanus niloticus
children.description: Africa's longest lizard; powerful swimmer; uses tools to raid crocodile nests; invasive in Florida.

children.name: Varanus salvator
children.description: Second-largest lizard; strong swimmer; found near water across SE Asia; eaten and traded widely.

children.name: Varanus varius
children.description: Arboreal predator of Australian eucalypt forests; raids bird nests; climbs telegraph poles.

children.name: Varanus griseus
children.description: Wide desert range from Sahara to Central Asia; tolerates extreme heat.

children.name: Varanus exanthematicus
children.description: Stocky; popular in captivity; some populations specialise on ants and termites.

children.name: Varanus acanthurus
children.description: Small spiny-tailed species of rocky Australian outback.

children.name: Varanus albigularis
children.description: Africa's largest monitor lizard, reaching up to 2 m; inhabits savannah and semi-arid scrub from southern Africa northward. A powerful predator that consumes tortoises, eggs, and carrion, using its strong jaws to crush shells.

children.name: Varanus bengalensis
children.description: One of the most widespread Asian monitors, ranging from Iran and Pakistan across the Indian subcontinent to SE Asia and southern China. Highly adaptable, occupying forest, farmland, and urban margins; a keystone species in many ecosystems where it controls rodent and insect populations.

children.name: Varanus beccarii
children.description: Entirely jet-black arboreal monitor from the Aru Islands of Indonesia, prized in the reptile hobby for its striking coloration. Reaches about 90–100 cm total length and lives in lowland rainforest canopy, feeding on insects, small vertebrates, and bird eggs.

children.name: Varanus caerulivirens
children.description: A strikingly coloured arboreal monitor described in 1999 from Halmahera and nearby North Maluku islands, Indonesia. Adults exhibit vivid turquoise-blue and black banding; inhabits lowland and hill rainforest canopy. Part of the prasinus tree-monitor complex.

children.name: Varanus boehmei
children.description: A small emerald-group tree monitor described in 2003 from Waigeo Island, West Papua, Indonesia. Lives in humid lowland rainforest; closely related to V. prasinus but distinguished by scale pattern and coloration.

children.name: Varanus bogerti
children.description: A melanistic tree monitor from the D'Entrecasteaux Islands and southeastern New Guinea, closely related to V. prasinus. Arboreal, insectivorous, and rarely seen in the wild; one of the darkest-coloured members of the emerald monitor group.

children.name: Varanus brevicauda
children.description: The world's smallest monitor lizard, reaching barely 20 cm total length; endemic to the arid spinifex grasslands of the Western Australian interior. Its proportionally short tail, unlike other monitors, stores fat reserves as an energy buffer during dry seasons.

children.name: Varanus bushi
children.description: A diminutive rock-dwelling monitor from the Pilbara and Kimberley regions of northwestern Australia, described in 2006. Closely related to V. acanthurus; inhabits rocky escarpments and spinifex grasslands.

children.name: Varanus caudolineatus
children.description: A small arboreal monitor of the mulga and spinifex scrublands of southern and western Australia, recognised by bold longitudinal stripes on its tail. Often shelters in hollow branches or under bark; feeds on insects, small lizards, and eggs.

children.name: Varanus cumingi
children.description: A large, semi-aquatic monitor endemic to the Philippines (Mindanao and adjacent islands), related to V. salvator. Inhabits lowland forests and riparian corridors; locally hunted for food and skin.

children.name: Varanus doreanus
children.description: A large monitor from New Guinea and the Cape York Peninsula of Australia, notable for its iridescent blue tail in juveniles. Inhabits lowland and coastal forests near rivers; closely related to V. indicus.

children.name: Varanus dumerilii
children.description: A stocky, orange-headed monitor of the rainforests and mangroves of the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Juveniles are spectacularly patterned in orange and black; adults fade to dark grey but retain the distinctive orange-red head.

children.name: Varanus eremius
children.description: A small, fast-running monitor of the red sand deserts and spinifex hummocks of central and western Australia. Rust-red to orange-brown dorsal colouration provides camouflage in ironstone-rich desert soils.

children.name: Varanus finschi
children.description: A mid-sized monitor from the Admiralty Islands and northern New Guinea, related to the V. indicus complex. Inhabits coastal forest and mangrove edges; described in 1994 after long confusion with other Melanesian species.

children.name: Varanus flavescens
children.description: A stocky, yellowish monitor of the alluvial plains of Pakistan, northern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh, closely associated with seasonally flooded grasslands and agricultural land. Listed on CITES Appendix I; threatened by habitat loss and collection for the leather trade.

children.name: Varanus giganteus
children.description: Australia's largest lizard and the fourth-largest extant lizard, reaching up to 2.5 m; inhabits desert ranges and rocky outcrops of central and western Australia. A formidable predator of small mammals, birds, and other lizards; revered in Aboriginal culture.

children.name: Varanus gilleni
children.description: A small, highly arboreal monitor of the mulga woodland belt of central and western Australia, spending most of its life in tree hollows and Acacia bark. Prehensile tail assists climbing; feeds primarily on invertebrates and small lizards.

children.name: Varanus glauerti
children.description: A slender, long-tailed monitor restricted to the rugged sandstone gorges and escarpments of the Kimberley region in northwestern Australia. Highly agile climber; feeds on insects and small vertebrates among rocky crevices.

children.name: Varanus glebopalma
children.description: A slender saxicolous monitor from the rocky escarpments and gorges of northern Australia (Kimberley, Arnhem Land). Has enlarged subdigital scales for gripping rock surfaces; often sympatric with V. glauerti.

children.name: Varanus gouldii
children.description: One of Australia's most widespread and familiar monitors, found across most of the continent in diverse habitats from tropical savannah to semi-arid scrubland. A powerful digging predator; it runs bipedally when alarmed and communicates via tail-waving.

children.name: Varanus hamersleyensis
children.description: A recently described (2014) monitor restricted to the iron-rich Hamersley Range of the Pilbara, Western Australia. Inhabits rocky gorges and spinifex grasslands; related to V. acanthurus; dark reddish-brown ground colour matches local ironstone.

children.name: Varanus indicus
children.description: Widespread and semi-aquatic throughout coastal New Guinea, eastern Indonesia, and northern Australia; an adept swimmer found in mangroves, reefs, and riparian forest. One of the most variable monitors in the Pacific region; the type species of the V. indicus complex.

children.name: Varanus jobiensis
children.description: A slender, semi-aquatic monitor from Yapen (Jobi) Island and surrounding New Guinea, with a distinctive pinkish throat. Inhabits lowland rainforest rivers and swamps; related to the V. indicus group.

children.name: Varanus juxtindicus
children.description: A large monitor endemic to Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands, described in 2002. Closely related to V. indicus; inhabits dense forest on this raised-coral island; very poorly known in the wild.

children.name: Varanus keithhornei
children.description: A large, brilliantly spotted arboreal monitor restricted to the wet tropical rainforest of Cape York Peninsula, Queensland. Rarely descends to the ground; bright yellow ocelli contrast strongly with a dark brown body.

children.name: Varanus kingorum
children.description: One of Australia's smallest monitors, reaching only about 23 cm; found in rocky outcrops and sandstone escarpments in the Northern Territory and Kimberley. Inhabits crevices that it defends as refugia against predators.

children.name: Varanus kordensis
children.description: A small arboreal monitor from Yapen Island (Biak/Kordos group) in the Cenderawasih Bay, West Papua. Green with black reticulation; closely allied to V. prasinus but recognised as a distinct species since 2002.

children.name: Varanus lirungensis
children.description: A medium-sized monitor described in 2009 from Lirung Island and the Talaud Archipelago of North Sulawesi, Indonesia. Member of the V. indicus complex; inhabits coastal and lowland forest.

children.name: Varanus mabitang
children.description: A very large, entirely black monitor endemic to the forests of Panay Island in the Philippines, described in 2001. Primarily frugivorous — one of only two monitor species known to eat significant quantities of fruit; critically threatened by deforestation and hunting.

children.name: Varanus macraei
children.description: A spectacularly coloured arboreal monitor from Batanta Island, West Papua, with electric-blue spots on a jet-black background. Described in 2001; lives in lowland rainforest and is highly sought after in the exotic pet trade.

children.name: Varanus melinus
children.description: A large, golden-yellow monitor from the Moluccas (Obi and Sula Islands), Indonesia, described in 1997. Reaches over 1.5 m; inhabits coastal and lowland forests; the vivid quince-yellow coloration fades with age.

children.name: Varanus mertensi
children.description: Australia's most aquatic monitor, inhabiting the rivers, billabongs, and waterholes of the tropical north. A powerful swimmer, using its laterally compressed tail as a sculling oar; feeds heavily on fish and aquatic invertebrates.

children.name: Varanus mitchelli
children.description: A semi-aquatic monitor of the freshwater wetlands and rivers of Australia's tropical north. Distinguished by numerous pale blue or white ocelli on a dark background; often basks on rocks or logs overhanging water.

children.name: Varanus namoroka
children.description: A small, slender monitor restricted to the tsingy limestone karst of the Namoroka National Park in northwestern Madagascar; one of only two Varanus species in Madagascar. Threatened by very limited range and habitat degradation.

children.name: Varanus nebulosus
children.description: A medium-sized monitor of the forests and woodland edges of the Indochinese Peninsula (Myanmar to peninsular Thailand and Malaysia). The clouded pattern of pale ocelli on a dark background distinguishes it from the closely related V. bengalensis.

children.name: Varanus nuchalis
children.description: A Philippine monitor lizard closely allied to V. salvator, from the Sulu Archipelago and Mindanao. Inhabits lowland forest and coastal areas; named for the distinctive scale pattern on its nape.

children.name: Varanus obor
children.description: A medium-sized monitor from Sanana Island, North Maluku, Indonesia, described in 2010. Member of the V. indicus group; 'obor' means 'torch' in reference to the bright yellow markings against a dark body.

children.name: Varanus olivaceus
children.description: A large, primarily frugivorous monitor from the rainforests of Luzon and Polillo Islands, Philippines — one of only two herbivorous monitors. Up to 2 m long; locally called 'butaan'; was thought extinct until its rediscovery near Manila in 1976. Endangered by deforestation and hunting.

children.name: Varanus ornatus
children.description: West and Central Africa's counterpart to the Nile Monitor; inhabits rainforest margins, gallery forest, and cocoa plantations from Senegal to Uganda. Often treated as V. niloticus ornatus; distinguished by its more vivid yellow-and-black pattern.

children.name: Varanus panoptes
children.description: A large, fast and aggressive monitor of open savannah and floodplains across tropical northern Australia and southern New Guinea. Highly visual predator; runs bipedally at speed; named for the many-eyed giant of Greek mythology due to its spotted pattern.

children.name: Varanus pilbarensis
children.description: A slender, pale monitor confined to the rocky gorges and spinifex ranges of the Pilbara region, Western Australia. Climbs expertly on smooth ironstone surfaces; closely related to V. acanthurus.

children.name: Varanus prasinus
children.description: The iconic, vividly green arboreal monitor of New Guinea's rainforest canopy, with a strongly prehensile tail used for gripping branches. Reaches about 1 m total length; the type species of the prasinus complex; eats insects, small vertebrates, and eggs in the forest canopy.

children.name: Varanus primordius
children.description: A tiny monitor of the tropical savannah woodlands and rocky outcrops of the Northern Territory, one of the smallest Australian varanids. Dark reddish-brown with small pale ocelli; shelters in rock crevices and termite mounds.

children.name: Varanus rainerguentheri
children.description: A large monitor from Halmahera and adjacent North Maluku islands, Indonesia, described in 2007. Member of the V. indicus complex; distinguished by dark dorsal ground colour with small pale spots.

children.name: Varanus rosenbergi
children.description: A large monitor of the mallee heathlands and woodlands of southern Australia; excavates termite mounds in which females lay eggs. Notable for using termite mounds as regulated-temperature incubators; listed as Near Threatened due to habitat fragmentation.

children.name: Varanus rudicollis
children.description: A slender, dramatically long-necked monitor of the lowland and hill rainforests of the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Borneo, and Sumatra. The rough, enlarged scales on the neck are diagnostic; a reclusive species rarely seen despite its sizeable range.

children.name: Varanus salvadorii
children.description: Papua New Guinea's apex lizard predator; arguably the world's longest lizard with confirmed lengths over 2.4 m, most of it tail. A powerful arboreal specialist with curved teeth for gripping live prey; feared in local folklore.

children.name: Varanus scalaris
children.description: A medium-sized arboreal monitor widespread in Timor, Flores, and surrounding Lesser Sunda Islands, as well as the Kimberley. Bold black-and-yellow banded or spotted pattern; agile climber of monsoon forests and woodland.

children.name: Varanus semiremex
children.description: A small, semi-aquatic monitor of the coastal mangroves and salt marshes of eastern Queensland, Australia. Rust-orange colouration; closely associated with mangroves; feeds on crabs and other invertebrates in tidal zones.

children.name: Varanus similis
children.description: A medium-sized monitor from the Bismarck Archipelago and the Solomon Islands, part of the V. indicus complex. Inhabits lowland coastal forest and mangroves; superficially similar to V. indicus, hence its Latin name.

children.name: Varanus sparnus
children.description: A very small monitor described in 2014 from the Dampier Peninsula and adjacent northwest Australia. The smallest member of the acanthurus group; inhabits rocky spinifex terrain.

children.name: Varanus spenceri
children.description: A large, ground-dwelling monitor of the black-soil floodplains and Mitchell grass downs of central-northern Australia. A powerful burrower; limited to seasonally flooded plains where it excavates deep refugia.

children.name: Varanus spinulosus
children.description: A large monitor endemic to the Solomons (New Georgia group), one of the most isolated Pacific monitor populations. Related to V. indicus; inhabits lowland forest of these remote western Solomon Islands.

children.name: Varanus storri
children.description: A small monitor of the tropical and semi-arid woodlands of northern and central Australia, named after prominent Australian herpetologist Glen Storr. Shelters in grass tussocks and rock crevices; one of Australia's most commonly encountered dwarf monitors.

children.name: Varanus telenesetes
children.description: A monitor lizard endemic to Rossel Island in the Louisiade Archipelago, easternmost Papua New Guinea, described in 1991. Very poorly known; related to V. bogerti; inhabits the island's rainforest interior.

children.name: Varanus timorensis
children.description: A small arboreal monitor from Timor and surrounding Lesser Sunda Islands (Flores, Samba); closely related to V. scalaris. Inhabits dry forests and monsoon woodland; the first monitor described from Timor.

children.name: Varanus togianus
children.description: A large, dark-coloured monitor from the Togian Islands of Central Sulawesi, Indonesia. Related to V. salvator; semi-aquatic habits; the Togian Island group, where it is endemic, is a biodiversity hotspot.

children.name: Varanus tristis
children.description: A widespread arboreal monitor with a distinctive dark or black head, found across northern and western Australia in woodland and rocky country. Highly agile tree-climber; will flee to the tree canopy when threatened.

children.name: Varanus yemenensis
children.description: A medium-sized monitor endemic to the mountains and wadis of Yemen and adjacent southwestern Saudi Arabia; one of the most range-restricted monitors in the Arabian Peninsula. Inhabits rocky arid hillsides and dry riverbeds at elevation.

children.name: Varanus flavirufus
children.description: A sandy or reddish-orange monitor widespread across the interior of Australia, formerly treated as a subspecies of V. gouldii. Favours red sand dunes and spinifex desert; an active forager that digs for buried prey including lizards and invertebrates.

children.name: Varanus caspius
children.description: A large, grey monitor of the arid lowlands and semi-desert east of the Caspian Sea, ranging through Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan into Afghanistan and Iran. Sometimes treated as a subspecies of V. griseus; one of Central Asia's largest reptiles.

children.name: Varanus douarrha
children.description: A large semi-aquatic monitor from New Ireland and New Britain in the Bismarck Archipelago, part of the V. indicus complex. Inhabits lowland coastal and riverine forest; previously confused with V. indicus until better specimens revealed its distinctness.

children.name: Varanus bitatawa
children.description: A large, primarily frugivorous monitor described in 2010 from the Sierra Madre mountains of northern Luzon, Philippines. Secretive; forest-dwelling; reaches nearly 2 m but was unknown to science until 2010 despite being known to indigenous hunters for generations.

children.name: Varanus bangonorum
children.description: A Philippine monitor described in 2014, found on Mindanao and nearby islands. Part of the V. salvator complex; inhabits lowland forest and riparian zones. Named after the Bangon people of Mindanao.

children.name: Varanus marmoratus
children.description: A large monitor from the Philippines (Mindanao, Leyte, Samar, Negros), part of the V. salvator complex. The bold marbled pattern of cream on dark brown is diagnostic; inhabits forest and riverine habitat.

children.name: Varanus palawanensis
children.description: A monitor lizard of the Palawan island group in the western Philippines, described in 2010 as distinct from V. salvator. Inhabits mangrove and lowland forest; semi-aquatic and locally hunted for food and skin trade.

children.name: Varanus samarensis
children.description: A monitor described in 2010 from Samar and Leyte Islands in the central Philippines, separated from V. salvator. Inhabits lowland tropical forest and mangroves; contributes to the high monitor diversity of the Philippines.

children.name: Varanus dalubhasa
children.description: A recently described (2014) Philippine monitor from northern Luzon, part of the V. salvator complex. Inhabits riparian forest; the species name means 'expert' or 'professor' in Filipino, reflecting the animal's known intelligence.

children.name: Varanus yuwonoi
children.description: A large, strikingly patterned monitor from Halmahera in the North Maluku islands, described in 1998. Three-part coloration — black, yellow, and reddish — makes it one of the most colourful of all varanids.

children.name: Varanus zugorum
children.description: A monitor from the Moluccas (Ternate and Tidore Islands) of Indonesia, described in 2005. Member of the V. indicus group; dark dorsum with pale ocellate spots; very poorly known in the wild.

children.name: Varanus reisingeri
children.description: A member of the V. prasinus tree-monitor complex, described in 2005 from Misool Island, West Papua. Bright green with yellowish ventral surface; lives in lowland rainforest canopy.

children.name: Varanus ocreatus
children.description: A small monitor of the rocky escarpments and sandy savannah of the Kimberley region of Western Australia. Close relative of V. storri; distinguished by pale-spotted colouration and preference for sandstone outcrops.

children.name: Varanus chlorostigma
children.description: A large monitor from Timor and the Lesser Sunda Islands, related to V. varius. Reaches 1.5–2 m; grey-brown with pale spots forming an ocellate pattern; inhabits dry monsoon forest and rocky hillsides in the Wallacean transition zone.
